hints.text: Exchange business cards with both hands, bow slightly, and take a moment to read the card before putting it away — never slip it into your pocket immediately.
hints.expertNote: Treat the card as an extension of the person, not a flyer.

hints.text: In meetings, let the senior person speak first. Nodding shows active listening, but avoid interrupting even when you disagree.
Arrive exactly on time — not early, not late. A handshake should be firm, brief, and accompanied by direct eye contact.
hints.expertNote: Punctuality signals respect for others' schedules.

hints.text: Germans value directness. A blunt 'no' is not rude — it is clarity. Avoid vague promises you cannot keep.
Expect warm greetings — a handshake plus light cheek kisses among colleagues who know each other. Personal rapport often precedes business talk.
Relationships matter more than contracts in early stages. Invest time in coffee chats and small talk before pushing terms.
hints.expertNote: Trust is the real currency in Brazilian deal-making.

hints.text: Present your business card with both hands, Chinese side facing up if bilingual. Address people by title and surname unless invited to use first names.
At a business banquet, wait for the host to start eating and to propose the first toast. Leave a little food on your plate — finishing everything can imply you were underfed.
Greet with 'Namaste' or a light handshake. Hierarchy matters — acknowledge the most senior person first in group introductions.
A head wobble can mean yes, acknowledgment, or polite uncertainty — not always agreement. Confirm decisions in writing.
hints.expertNote: Verbal yes and practical yes are not always the same.

hints.text: Dress slightly more formal than you might at home. Lunch meetings are common and can last two hours — rushing signals disrespect.
Debate is intellectual, not personal. Challenging ideas in a meeting can be a sign of engagement, not hostility.
Bow when greeting, with depth matching the other person's seniority. Offer and receive business cards with both hands.
Never pour your own drink — pour for others and let them pour for you. Use both hands when giving or receiving a glass from a senior colleague.
A handshake is standard; closer contacts may hug. Use titles (Licenciado, Ingeniero) until told otherwise.
Indirect refusals are common to save face. Phrases like 'we will see' or 'it is difficult' often mean no — read between the lines.
hints.expertNote: Listen for what is not said.

hints.text: If offering a gift, avoid alcohol or pork-related items. Present it with the right hand or both hands and do not expect it to be opened immediately.
Dress conservatively in business settings. Meetings may start with extended hospitality — accept coffee or dates before diving into agenda items.
Personal connection matters — expect conversation about family, food, or football before business. Rushing to the pitch can feel cold.
hints.expertNote: Build warmth before building the deal.

hints.text: A firm handshake with direct eye contact is standard. Address people by title plus first name (e.g. 'Mr. Ahmet') until invited to be more informal.
Business meals are social events — accept tea or coffee offers graciously. Refusing hospitality can be read as coldness, not efficiency.
Relationship-building (ta'arof) is essential before deals. Expect polite offers of gifts or favors — a graceful decline is often part of the ritual.
Avoid showing the sole of your foot, pointing with a finger, or offering a thumbs-up in formal settings. These gestures can be seen as disrespectful.
hints.expertNote: When unsure, mirror your host's body language.

hints.text: The wai greeting (palms together, slight bow) is appropriate in business — the height of your hands reflects the other person's status.
Never touch someone's head or point your feet at people or sacred objects. Criticizing the monarchy is illegal and deeply offensive.
